_meta.what_firm_does: Brex is a fintech (corporate cards, spend management, banking). This role is the operational backbone for GTM/SDR onboarding: running monthly new-hire cohorts, owning the GTM Enablement Calendar, provisioning tools, building SDR playbook materials, facilitating onboarding sessions, tracking ramp metrics.
_meta.jd_wants: 1-2 yrs frontline SDR experience w/ quota; strong communication + presentation; exceptional organization + attention to detail; SDR tech stack (Salesforce, ZoomInfo, Outreach, Orum, LinkedIn Sales Navigator); LMS; AI-tool experimentation; B2B SaaS/fintech a plus.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie does NOT have frontline SDR/quota experience; she has strong enablement/onboarding/training + organization. Answers are honest about the gap. Jamie to decide whether to apply.

Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is that it's enablement work made into a job—and enablement is where I do my best work. At InGenius Prep I built the onboarding processes and documentation that cut ramp time by 75%, I run end-to-end logistics for 20+ programs, and I facilitate training for 600+ people. The part I love most is the diagnosis: when a new sales process I rolled out didn't get adopted, I interviewed the reps, found the real blocker was friction, and built a one-pager that fixed it. A Sales Enablement Specialist role is exactly that instinct, applied to getting GTM hires productive faster.
Why do you want to work at Brex?: Two reasons. First, the work fits how I'm wired—I'm the person who keeps the operational engine of onboarding running cleanly, owns the calendar, and tracks whether people are actually ramping. My supervisor once called me the most organized person he's ever worked with, and that's the muscle this role lives on. Second, I want to grow in the GTM enablement space specifically, and Brex's pace and scale would stretch me. I'll be honest that I haven't carried an SDR quota, but the coordination, training, and measurement core of the role is what I've been doing for years.
How would you use data to improve onboar: I measure behavior, not just completion. At NextGen I analyzed 2,000+ employee experiences and turned the feedback into reports that changed strategy—so I'm comfortable letting data tell me what's actually working. For ramp, I'd track time-to-proficiency and where new hires stall, then go talk to the people stuck at that point rather than guessing. That diagnose-then-design loop is how I cut onboarding time 75% at InGenius. I'd also lean on AI tools—I use them daily and have been building a RAG-based system in my own time—to personalize ramp materials to where each hire actually needs help.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. In practice that means I diagnose with data where new hires struggle, collaborate cross-functionally on the fix, and track ramp metrics to make sure it holds. I'm honest about where I'm still building—frontline sales specifically—and equally confident in what I'd bring on day one: tight coordination, strong training delivery, and a genuine drive to make onboarding better.
